items.question_text: When do these route-distance limitations apply?

choices.text: When you clear an aircraft in controlled airspace via other than an established airway or route.
Whenever you issue any IFR clearance, including one entirely on established airways.
Only when the aircraft is below 3,000 feet above the surface.
Only for VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories.

items.explanation: The paragraph governs routes other than established airways or routes in controlled airspace.

items.question_text: Which case is an authorized exception to the route limitations in 4-1-1?

choices.text: A pilot requests VFR-on-top and the route segment does not meet the normal service-volume limitation.
The controller prefers to shorten the route even though the pilot did not request it.
The aircraft is late and the controller wants to recover schedule without coordination.
The route uses any convenient fix regardless of signal coverage.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-1-2 lists specific exceptions, including certain VFR-on-top situations and other approved cases.

items.question_text: If you clear an aircraft to cross or hold at a fix, what must the assigned altitude reflect?

choices.text: The limitations of the NAVAID or aid used to define that fix.
The pilot's preferred cruise altitude, even if the aid cannot support it.
Any altitude that keeps the aircraft within your sector long enough for coordination.
Only the minimum vectoring altitude, regardless of how the fix is defined.

items.explanation: Crossing or holding altitudes must remain consistent with the limitations of the aid used for the fix.

items.question_text: When may you clear an aircraft on a route segment that does not meet normal signal-service limitations under the VFR-on-top exception?

choices.text: Only when the pilot has requested VFR-on-top on that route.
Whenever the weather is VFR and the aircraft is radar identified.
Whenever the route is shorter than the filed route.
Only after the pilot cancels IFR.

items.explanation: The VFR-on-top exception is tied to a pilot request; it is not a general shortcut authority.

items.question_text: Which fix may be assigned to a TACAN-equipped aircraft that does not have VOR capability?

choices.text: A fix based on TACAN or VORTAC, because those are usable by the aircraft.
Any VOR-defined fix, even if the aircraft cannot use VOR.
Any unpublished point the controller can describe verbally.
The destination airport and no intermediate fixes.

items.explanation: Fix use must match the aircraft's navigation capability, so a TACAN-equipped aircraft without VOR capability needs TACAN-usable fixes.

items.question_text: If an IFR clearance limit is an intersection or waypoint and its type is known, what must the clearance include?

choices.text: The intersection or waypoint name followed by its type.
Only the coded computer fix name, with no type.
The nearest airport name instead of the fix type.
The fix name only if the pilot requests the type separately.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-1 requires known NAVAID, intersection, and waypoint types to be stated with the clearance limit.

items.question_text: A clearance is being relayed through a non-ATC facility. How should it be prefaced?

choices.text: With the appropriate prefix such as "ATC clears," "ATC advises," or "ATC requests."
With "for advisory use only," because the facility is not ATC.
With no prefix, because the message content speaks for itself.
With "pilot discretion," because the message is being relayed.

items.explanation: Relayed clearances, information, or requests through non-ATC facilities must carry the ATC prefix.

items.question_text: If special clearance delivery instructions are needed, what does the rule require?

choices.text: Issue the specific clearance delivery instructions when appropriate.
Avoid delivery instructions and let the next controller work them out.
Issue only the route and altitude; delivery instructions are never part of the clearance.
Delay all delivery instructions until after takeoff.

items.explanation: The paragraph is direct: if specific clearance delivery instructions are appropriate, issue them.

items.question_text: How must a clearance be relayed?

choices.text: Verbatim.
Paraphrased as long as the operational meaning seems unchanged.
Shortened to the route and altitude only.
Reworded in plain language for pilot convenience.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-4 requires clearances to be relayed verbatim.

items.question_text: What is one approved way to amend a previously issued route clearance?

choices.text: State the route amendment and then state that the rest of the route is unchanged.
Issue only the changed fix and assume the pilot knows where the original route resumes.
Restate only the destination airport and omit the route details.
Tell the pilot to expect a revised route from the next sector instead of amending the clearance.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-5 allows several specific amendment methods, including amending the route and stating the rest is unchanged.

items.question_text: What does the rule allow regarding intermediate stops?

choices.text: You may clear an aircraft through intermediate stops.
Intermediate stops always require separate IFR clearances for every leg.
Through clearances are limited to VFR aircraft.
A through clearance may be issued only if the aircraft remains on the ground at each stop for less than 30 minutes.

items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly authorizes clearing an aircraft through intermediate stops.

items.question_text: What phrase is used when an aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?

choices.text: "Via approved altitude reservation flight plan."
"Via military random route as filed."
"Via special use airspace clearance."
"Proceed in accordance with local reservation procedures."

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-7 specifies the ALTRV phrase to use in the clearance.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft below minimum IFR altitude requests an IFR clearance and cannot climb in VFR conditions to that minimum altitude. What must you determine before issuing the clearance?

choices.text: Whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.
Whether the pilot can climb at least 500 feet per minute regardless of terrain.
Whether another aircraft can relay the clearance on frequency.
Whether the pilot is willing to accept radar vectors below the MIA or MEA.

items.explanation: Pop-up IFR below minimum IFR altitude requires determining whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the proper minimum.

items.question_text: When processing an airfile aircraft, what must you verify before working the request unless other coordination already exists?

choices.text: That the aircraft is within your area of jurisdiction.
That the aircraft has already landed at the destination airport.
That the flight plan has priority over all other ATC duties.
That the pilot has already been assigned a STAR.

items.explanation: Airfile processing begins with jurisdiction and the information needed to provide IFR service.

items.question_text: Before sending an IFR arrival at a non-towered or non-FSS airport to CTAF, what must you do if needed?

choices.text: Provide the pilot with instructions on how to cancel the IFR flight plan.
Tell the pilot the IFR flight plan cancels automatically upon landing everywhere.
Require the pilot to remain on the approach frequency until shutdown.
Transfer cancellation responsibility to the next aircraft on frequency.

items.explanation: At airports not served by a tower or FSS, controllers must provide IFR cancellation instructions before sending the pilot to CTAF if needed.

items.question_text: How should the word "takeoff" be handled in departure phraseology?

choices.text: Use it only when actually issuing or canceling a takeoff clearance.
Use it in any departure advisory so the pilot understands the phase of flight.
Use it whenever an aircraft is number one for departure.
Avoid the word entirely, including actual takeoff clearances.

items.explanation: Controllers protect the word "takeoff" for the actual takeoff clearance or its cancellation.

items.question_text: When an IFR departure clearance is being relayed through an FSS or dispatcher, what must always be included?

choices.text: The airport of departure.
The pilot's fuel endurance.
The departure controller's initials.
A full weather briefing.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-3-2 specifically requires the airport of departure when the clearance is relayed by a nonpilot intermediary.

items.question_text: When may abbreviated departure clearance procedures be used?

choices.text: When the route and data still support abbreviation and the filed route has not been changed.
Whenever the controller wants to shorten the clearance, even if the route changed.
Only for VFR departures that will pick up IFR later.
Only when the pilot has already taxied onto the runway.

items.explanation: Abbreviated departure clearances are not for changed filed routes; the controller must ensure the abbreviated format still accurately conveys the clearance.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft departs from an airport without a tower. Which additional control action may be required before departure?

choices.text: Issue a departure release, hold-for-release, or release time as appropriate.
Issue a visual approach clearance before takeoff.
Cancel the IFR flight plan until the aircraft is airborne.
Require the pilot to self-release if no conflicting traffic is reported.

items.explanation: Untowered IFR departures may require a release, hold-for-release, or release time under paragraph 4-3-4.

items.question_text: A ground stop applies to an aircraft ready for release. What is the rule?

choices.text: Do not release the aircraft without approval from the originator of the ground stop.
Release the aircraft if the pilot accepts an expected departure delay.
Release the aircraft if the next facility agrees verbally, regardless of the originator.
Convert the flight to VFR departure automatically.

items.explanation: Ground stop relief must come from the originator; the aircraft may not be released otherwise.

items.question_text: Several aircraft choose to absorb delay on the ground before departure. In what order should clearances normally be issued?

choices.text: In the order the original clearance requests were made, if practicable.
In order of aircraft speed so the fastest aircraft launches first.
In any order chosen by the tower as long as wake separation is maintained.
In reverse order so later requesters can catch up.

items.explanation: Ground-delay sequencing preserves the original request order when practicable.

items.question_text: What must you do when departure delays are anticipated?

choices.text: Inform the affected approach control facilities and/or towers of the anticipated delays.
Wait until every delayed aircraft has called for taxi before coordinating.
Coordinate only if a pilot specifically asks why the delay exists.
Issue the delays by NOTAM rather than controller-to-controller coordination.

items.explanation: Departure-delay information must be passed to the facilities that need it.

items.question_text: Absent an automation agreement that reduces the threshold, when must you coordinate with the receiving facility before departure?

choices.text: When the departure point is less than 15 minutes flying time from your facility boundary.
Whenever the destination is outside your facility, regardless of flying time.
Only when the aircraft is climbing above FL180.
Only after the pilot is handed off airborne.

items.explanation: The baseline coordination trigger is less than 15 minutes to the boundary unless automation or agreement reduces it.

items.question_text: A pilot with an IFR flight plan asks to depart VFR and pick up the clearance later. If approval is obtained, what must you provide?

choices.text: Authorize the VFR departure and give the proper frequency and, if needed, where or when to contact for the IFR clearance.
Issue the full IFR clearance but tell the pilot to disregard it until desired.
Cancel the flight plan and tell the pilot to refile after departure.
Keep the pilot on the current frequency until reaching cruise altitude.

items.explanation: VFR release of an IFR departure includes the authorization plus the contact instructions for obtaining the IFR clearance.

items.question_text: Unless automation or a letter of agreement says otherwise, where must a terminal facility forward departure times?

choices.text: To the facility that issued the clearance and to the terminal departure controller when that position is involved.
Only to the destination tower.
Only to the overlying center, regardless of who issued the clearance.
Only to flight service, because they maintain the legal departure record.

items.explanation: Departure times must be forwarded to the clearance-originating facility and the terminal departure controller when involved, unless alternate procedures apply.

items.question_text: When clearing aircraft via routes, what principle governs the route you choose?

choices.text: The route must be consistent with the altitude stratum in which the aircraft will operate.
Any published route may be used at any altitude if the pilot agrees.
Random RNAV routes replace all ATS route requirements for every aircraft.
Controllers may ignore route structure whenever a direct route seems shorter.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-1 ties route assignment to the altitude stratum being used.

items.question_text: An RNAV aircraft is transitioning between ATS routes via a random RNAV segment. What additional service is required?

choices.text: Provide radar monitoring during the random RNAV transition.
Require the pilot to cancel RNAV capability and navigate by raw data only.
Convert the flight to nonradar handling during the transition.
Issue no altitude assignment until the aircraft rejoins a published route.

items.explanation: Random RNAV transitions between ATS routes require radar monitoring.

items.question_text: Below FL180, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes used to define a military route segment?

choices.text: 80 miles.
120 miles.
180 miles.
260 miles.

items.explanation: Degree-distance route segments below FL180 are limited to 80 miles between defining fixes.

items.question_text: When clearing a non-RNAV aircraft on a depicted alternative route because part of the airway is unusable, what word must immediately precede that route?

choices.text: "Substitute."
"Amended."
"Reroute."
"Bypass."

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-4 requires the word "substitute" when using a depicted alternative route.

items.question_text: When may an IFR route include Class G airspace?

choices.text: Only when the pilot requests it, including by filing a route that constitutes such a request.
Whenever the controller can save mileage by shortcutting through uncontrolled airspace.
Only when the weather is IMC throughout the segment.
Never; IFR routes may not include Class G airspace.

items.explanation: Routes through Class G airspace are pilot-request only, and some filed routes count as that request.

items.question_text: En route, when may you issue a revised direct routing that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in your facility's airspace?

choices.text: Only when the pilot requests it or operational necessity dictates it.
Any time the route is shorter than the filed route.
Whenever the aircraft is not part of a traffic management initiative.
Only after the aircraft reaches cruise altitude above FL180.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-6 limits direct reroutes past the last fix in your airspace unless the pilot requested it or operations require it.

items.question_text: What vertical separation minimum applies above FL410 in standard domestic operations unless a stated exception applies?

choices.text: 2,000 feet.
1,000 feet.
3,000 feet.
4,000 feet.

items.explanation: Above FL410, the basic vertical separation standard is 2,000 feet unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.question_text: At and below FL410, what altitude pattern applies to an aircraft on a magnetic course from 0 through 179 degrees?

choices.text: Odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels.
Even cardinal altitudes or flight levels.
Only altitudes ending in 500 feet.
Any altitude chosen by the pilot if radar identified.

items.explanation: Flight direction tables assign odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels to 0 through 179 degree courses at and below FL410.

items.question_text: When traffic conditions are the reason you want to disregard normal flight-direction altitudes, what approval is needed if the aircraft will proceed beyond your facility's area?

choices.text: The operation must be covered by a letter of agreement between the affected facilities.
No approval is needed once the pilot accepts the altitude.
Only the destination tower must agree.
A supervisor may approve it locally without any interfacility procedure.

items.explanation: Random altitude assignments beyond the facility area for traffic reasons require specific LOA coverage.

items.question_text: If the altimeter setting is 29.91, what is the lowest usable flight level you may clear at or above 18,000 feet MSL?
items.explanation: Table 4-5-22 makes FL190 the lowest usable flight level when the altimeter setting is 29.91 to 28.92.

items.question_text: If the minimum IFR altitude is at or above 18,000 feet MSL and the altimeter setting is below 29.92, how is the adjusted minimum flight level determined?

choices.text: Add the table's adjustment factor to the flight-level equivalent of the minimum altitude.
Subtract 500 feet from the minimum altitude to compensate for low pressure.
Use FL180 regardless of the pressure setting once the route is in controlled airspace.
Convert the route to a MOCA-based clearance instead of using flight levels.

items.explanation: Adjusted minimum flight level comes from adding the table adjustment to the flight-level equivalent of the minimum altitude.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft be cleared below the MEA but not below the MOCA on the route segment being flown?

choices.text: Only when the paragraph's stated conditions are met, such as proper navigation capability or radar-procedure conditions.
Whenever the pilot reports clear weather below the MEA.
Any time the aircraft is within 50 miles of the destination airport.
Only after the pilot has the airport in sight.

items.explanation: Below-MEA clearances are limited exceptions; the aircraft still may not go below the MOCA and must meet the specific conditions in the paragraph.

items.question_text: If you issue a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach and an unpublished route will be used, what extra protection is normally required?

choices.text: An appropriate crossing altitude that ensures terrain clearance until the pilot reaches a point where altitude information is available.
A visual approach clearance before the cruise clearance becomes effective.
A hold-for-release time from the destination airport.
No extra protection is needed because cruise automatically guarantees terrain clearance everywhere.

items.explanation: When an unpublished route is involved, the controller must protect terrain clearance with a suitable crossing altitude until published guidance is available to the pilot.

items.question_text: What should you do when you know an aircraft will not be able to climb or descend immediately because another facility controls the blocking altitude?

choices.text: If practicable, tell the pilot when to expect the altitude change or request it from the controlling facility.
Wait silently until the altitude becomes available, because advance notice is discouraged.
Cancel the altitude request and require the pilot to ask again later.
Issue the climb or descent anyway and let the next facility resolve the conflict.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-5-8 emphasizes advising the pilot of anticipated altitude changes when practicable.

items.question_text: In nonradar operations, when is altitude confirmation normally required?

choices.text: On initial contact and position reports unless one of the listed exceptions applies.
Only after the aircraft reports leaving the final approach fix.
Only for VFR-on-top aircraft.
Never, because nonradar procedures assume altitude compliance without confirmation.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-5-9 requires altitude confirmation in nonradar operations unless an exception applies.

items.question_text: What must a clearance to a holding fix include when delay is expected?

choices.text: The clearance limit, holding instructions, and an expect-further-clearance time.
Only the fix name and the direction of turns.
Only the route and the next center frequency.
A radar vector plan in place of holding instructions.

items.explanation: A holding clearance to a fix must contain the limit, the holding instructions, and the EFC when delay exists.

items.question_text: When should a clearance beyond a holding fix normally be issued?

choices.text: As soon as possible, preferably at least five minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.
Only after the aircraft has completed one full holding pattern.
No earlier than the published expect-further-clearance time.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for onward routing.

items.explanation: Controllers should issue the beyond-fix clearance as soon as possible, preferably at least five minutes before the fix.

items.question_text: Arrival delays are expected to reach 30 minutes. What is one required action?

choices.text: Issue total delay information as soon as possible, preferably by the first controller who talks to the aircraft.
Wait until the aircraft is established in holding before mentioning the delay.
Issue only the reason for the delay; the total delay time is not required.
Provide delay information only if the pilot has already copied the ATIS.

items.explanation: Once delays reach or are expected to reach 30 minutes, total delay information must be issued promptly unless the ATIS exception applies.

items.question_text: Which item must be specified when issuing holding instructions?

choices.text: The course, radial, bearing, track, azimuth, airway, or route on which the aircraft is to hold.
The next facility's handoff frequency.
The runway expected after landing.
A mandatory right-turn pattern in every case.

items.explanation: Holding instructions must specify the basic holding pattern elements, including the course or route on which the hold is flown.

items.question_text: When may a visual holding point be used?

choices.text: Only when the pilot can determine the point by visual reference and is familiar enough with it to use it reliably.
Whenever the controller can see the aircraft and point from the tower cab.
Only at airports with radar service.
Any time the holding point is within five miles of the airport.

items.explanation: Visual holding points depend on the pilot's ability to identify and use the point by visual reference.

items.question_text: A pilot requests to deviate from the normal holding flight path. When may you approve it?

choices.text: When obstacles and traffic permit the deviation.
Whenever the pilot says the request is for convenience.
Only if the pilot agrees to remain above the MEA.
Never; holding flight path deviations are prohibited.

items.explanation: Holding-path deviations may be approved only when traffic and obstacle considerations allow it.

items.question_text: What must be protected for an aircraft holding on an unmonitored NAVAID when no signal is being received?

choices.text: The holding course associated with that unmonitored NAVAID.
Only the aircraft's last radar track, because the fix can no longer be used.
Only the inbound route beyond the hold; the holding pattern itself is no longer protected.
Nothing, because the pilot is solely responsible once the signal is lost.

items.explanation: Controllers still protect the holding course for aircraft assigned to hold on an unmonitored NAVAID.

items.question_text: Under paragraph 4-6-8, when does ILS critical-area protection become especially relevant to holding operations?

choices.text: When low ceiling or visibility conditions exist and the hold could affect the localizer or critical area environment.
Only when the airport is VFR and visual approaches are in use.
Only for helicopters conducting practice approaches.
Never; holding and ILS protection are unrelated.

items.explanation: The paragraph protects ILS use in lower weather conditions when holding operations could interfere with the system or critical area.

items.question_text: When assigning a STAR or RNAV STAR in an arrival clearance, what must the clearance include?

choices.text: The STAR name and transition, if needed, to be flown.
Only the runway number; the STAR name may be omitted.
Only the destination airport because the pilot already filed the arrival.
The missed approach procedure for the expected instrument approach.

items.explanation: Arrival clearances using a STAR or RNAV STAR must state the name and transition when necessary.

items.question_text: What must be true before issuing an advance descent clearance coordinated with an adjacent center?

choices.text: The receiving facility must be coordinated with, and there must be enough distance to accomplish the descent safely.
The pilot must cancel IFR before crossing the center boundary.
The aircraft must already be within 10 miles of the airport.
No coordination is required if the pilot asks first.

items.explanation: Advance descent clearances depend on both coordination and adequate distance for the descent.

items.question_text: In a single frequency approach environment, what is the normal rule after the approach has begun or the initial en route descent contact has been made?

choices.text: Do not change the aircraft's frequency unless one of the stated exceptions applies.
Change the frequency at every phase boundary so each controller handles only one phase.
Transfer the aircraft to tower as soon as it is descending below the MVA.
Cancel radar service and use CTAF procedures in place of further ATC contact.

items.explanation: Single frequency approaches are designed to avoid unnecessary frequency changes once the approach sequence has effectively begun.

items.question_text: What is the controller's objective for radio frequency and beacon-code changes involving military single-pilot turbojet aircraft?

choices.text: Minimize those changes, especially below 2,500 feet AGL and during turns or critical maneuvering.
Increase them during descent so each sector has a clean handoff record.
Delay all beacon assignments until final approach.
Use no radar beacon code whenever the aircraft is on an instrument approach.

items.explanation: The paragraph focuses on reducing cockpit workload for military single-pilot turbojets by minimizing frequency and beacon changes.

items.question_text: What is the purpose of the special military turbojet en route descent guidance for arrivals?

choices.text: To manage approach expectations and descent handling for military turbojet arrivals under the paragraph's special procedures.
To authorize visual approaches without weather minimums for military aircraft.
To remove the need to issue altimeter settings to military aircraft.
To require every military turbojet to cancel IFR before entering terminal airspace.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-5 provides specific descent and approach-expectation handling for military turbojet arrivals.

items.question_text: What information must an en route facility forward to a nonapproach control tower for an inbound IFR arrival?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type, ETA, and the approach procedure the aircraft will execute.
Only the aircraft call sign and requested parking gate.
Only the assigned transponder code and destination runway.
Only the pilot's weather request and alternate airport.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-6 lists the arrival data en route facilities forward to towers or FSSs for inbound aircraft.

items.question_text: When must an en route controller transmit weather to an arriving aircraft before or as part of the approach clearance?

choices.text: When an available official report is below the stated ceiling or visibility thresholds and the report is being passed directly or through an authorized relay path.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for a weather update after receiving the approach clearance.
Whenever the airport has any precipitation, regardless of ceiling or visibility.
Only for military aircraft on precision approaches.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-7 ties the transmission requirement to specific low-weather thresholds and the controller's direct or relayed communication path.

items.question_text: An arriving pilot reports weather below the aircraft's landing minima. What should you do?

choices.text: Issue instructions to hold or proceed to another airport and resequence other aircraft as needed.
Continue the approach because the reported airport weather controls, not the pilot's minima.
Cancel IFR and tell the pilot to decide without further ATC involvement.
Issue a visual approach if the pilot has the runway environment in sight.

items.explanation: Below-minima pilot reports require holding or diversion instructions and traffic-sequence adjustment as necessary.

items.question_text: Why must transfer of jurisdiction to the approach control facility be made early enough on an arrival?

choices.text: So the receiving facility has time to clear the aircraft beyond its clearance limit.
So the center can stop tracking the aircraft before descent begins.
So the pilot can choose the runway before talking to approach.
So tower can issue the missed approach procedure directly.

items.explanation: The transfer must occur early enough for the receiving facility to continue the arrival clearance process beyond the limit.

items.question_text: If the destination airport has no ATIS and the pilot has not reported receiving automated weather, which items are part of the required approach information package?

choices.text: Surface wind, ceiling and visibility when below the stated threshold, and the altimeter setting.
Only the runway assignment and missed approach procedure.
Only the name of the airport and the nearest FSS.
No weather items are required if the controller expects a straight-in approach.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-10 specifies the approach information items that must be issued when they are not covered by ATIS or automated weather acknowledgment.

items.question_text: When a tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, what arrival information must be forwarded to the tower?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type if needed for separation, and the approach procedure or runway if it differs from the one in use.
Only the aircraft identification because the tower can infer the rest.
Only the aircraft type and transponder code.
Only the ETA and parking stand assignment.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-11 lists the tower-arrival data to be forwarded when the tower and TRACON are collocated.

items.question_text: For en route approach control service, which item is specifically excluded from the airport conditions information package?

choices.text: Taxiway, apron, or service-area NOTAM information outside the landing area itself.
Known abnormal operation of approach or landing aids.
Destination airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing.
Runway condition information affecting the landing area.

items.explanation: The note under paragraph 4-7-12 excludes airport surface-environment items outside the landing area from en route airport-conditions information.

items.question_text: When a terminal facility changes from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously, what must be done?

choices.text: Coordinate with the facilities that use fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.
Advise pilots only; no facility coordination is needed.
Cancel all arrivals until the new ILS is active for 30 minutes.
Treat the change as a tower-only action that does not affect other facilities.

items.explanation: Switching among non-simultaneous ILS systems requires coordination with facilities that depend on the related fixes.

items.question_text: Where must an instrument approach procedure begin unless the charted procedure provides an intermediate-fix start because there is no IAF?

choices.text: At an initial approach fix, or at an intermediate fix only if there is no IAF.
At any point on final once the aircraft is aligned with the runway.
At the final approach fix regardless of the published procedure.
At the decision altitude if the pilot is in radar contact.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-1 requires the approach to begin at the IAF or, if none exists, the IF.

items.question_text: What extra identification must appear in an approach clearance to an uncontrolled airport or an airport with a closed tower?

choices.text: The airport name.
The CTAF frequency in place of the airport name.
The parking stand or FBO name.
The runway magnetic heading.

items.explanation: Approach clearances to uncontrolled or closed-tower airports must include the airport name.

items.question_text: If an approach clearance is relayed, what additional information must accompany it when required and available?

choices.text: The weather information required for the approach or arrival, when it is required and available.
Only the aircraft's filed alternate airport.
The transponder code to be used after landing.
No additional information; the relayed clearance stands alone in every case.

items.explanation: Relayed approach clearances must still include required weather or other necessary associated information when available.

items.question_text: When may a controller assign an altitude above the one published for a military high altitude instrument approach?

choices.text: When a higher altitude is required for separation.
Whenever the pilot requests more time to set up the approach.
Only when the glideslope is out of service.
Never; the charted altitude may not be changed.

items.explanation: Military high altitude approach altitudes may be assigned above chart values when separation requires it.

items.question_text: When must the approach clearance state the charted altitude?

choices.text: When adherence to that published altitude is required for separation.
In every approach clearance without exception.
Only when the pilot says he or she is unfamiliar with the procedure.
Only at uncontrolled airports.

items.explanation: The altitude is specified in the clearance only when pilot adherence to that published altitude is needed for separation.

items.question_text: What restriction applies to circling approach instructions?

choices.text: They may be given only for aircraft landing at airports with operational control towers.
They may be issued at any airport as long as the weather is VFR.
They require the pilot to remain outside the published circling area until the runway is in sight.
They may always include extended downwind instructions beyond the circling area.

items.explanation: Circling instructions are limited to airports with operational towers, and the controller may not direct flight outside the protected circling area.

items.question_text: When may a side-step maneuver be authorized?

choices.text: Only when the maneuver is authorized by the published procedure.
Whenever the pilot sees the parallel runway and requests it.
Only when both runways have the same instrument approach.
Any time the tower is open and the weather is above basic VFR.

items.explanation: Side-step authorization is limited to procedures that specifically allow it.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft on approach be released to change to the advisory frequency?

choices.text: When direct communications are no longer needed.
Immediately after the approach clearance is issued, regardless of need.
Only after the aircraft lands.
Never; IFR aircraft may not change to advisory frequency before landing.

items.explanation: Communications release depends on whether continued direct communications are still needed.

items.question_text: What does an approach clearance automatically authorize in the normal IFR case?

choices.text: Execution of the published missed approach procedure for the instrument approach being flown.
Any alternate missed approach the pilot prefers.
A visual maneuver to any runway once the runway is in sight.
A turn before the missed approach point whenever the pilot sees fit.

items.explanation: Except for a VFR aircraft practicing an instrument approach, the approach clearance carries the published missed approach automatically.

items.question_text: A pilot says they are unfamiliar with the approach. Which item is part of the extra information you must provide?

choices.text: The initial approach altitude.
The aircraft's exact distance from every weather reporting point on the airport.
The local terrain elevation on each side of the final approach course.
The departure frequency to use after landing.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-10 lists extra approach-briefing items for unfamiliar pilots, including the initial approach altitude and other procedure details.

items.question_text: What separation rule applies to an IFR aircraft conducting a practice instrument approach?

choices.text: It must be afforded IFR separation until it lands and terminates the flight or cancels the IFR flight plan.
It may be treated like a VFR practice aircraft once established on final approach.
It needs IFR separation only if the ceiling is below 1,000 feet.
It loses IFR separation the moment the pilot reports the airport in sight.

items.explanation: IFR practice approaches still receive IFR separation until the flight is terminated or the pilot cancels IFR.

items.question_text: When must climb-out or other departure instructions be issued to an aircraft cleared for a low approach, touch-and-go, or practice approach?

choices.text: Before the aircraft begins its final descent.
After the aircraft touches down or crosses the landing threshold.
Only if the pilot asks for them on short final.
Only after tower observes the go-around in progress.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-12 requires the departure instructions to be issued before the final descent begins.
